title: Executive Order 9661—Possession, Control, and Operation of the Transportation System, Plants, and Facilities of the Great Lakes Towing Company, Cleveland, Ohio

flags.justification: The Director of the Office of Defense Transportation is authorized to operate the seized system 'in such a manner as he may deem necessary' — broad, open discretion with no external check.

Sec. 4: possession terminates 'when he determines that such possession... are no longer necessary' — self-assessed, with no outer time bound, unlike several sibling seizure orders in this corpus with explicit 60-day caps.
Single purpose: seizure of this one company's transportation system.
Sec. 2 requires operation under 'terms and conditions of employment in effect at the time possession is taken' — a real status-quo constraint.
No incentive structure rewarding behavior contrary to the order's purpose is identifiable.
No outer time bound; termination rests entirely on the Director's own self-assessment, consistent with the Flag 2 finding.

Sec. 3 explicitly blocks ordinary judicial process: 'Except with the prior written consent of the Director, no attachment by mesne process, garnishment, execution, or otherwise shall be levied' — the same affirmative closure of legal remedy pathways already established as CRITICAL in EO 9570 and EO 9341.
Combined with the judicial-process block (Flag 7) and the absence of any outer time bound (Flag 2/6), no external party has any meaningful avenue to check this seizure's exercise or duration.
By November 1945 this specific pattern, including the mesne-process-blocking clause, is an established, repeated template (see EO 9341, EO 9570) rather than a novel authority claim.
Sec. 6's request-based cooperation with the Secretary of the Navy for protection and resources is coordination, not mandate redirection.
No coverage population or exemption structure is created.

notes: Validation batch (positive). Structural weight: 6/20 = 30.0%. Notably less protective than its better-designed sibling seizure orders (e.g. EO 9474, EO 9639) — no 60-day cap, and shares the judicial-process-blocking clause with EO 9341 and EO 9570.
